sentences.text: My name is Susan Rapaport-- R-A-P-A-P-O-R-T.
My name was Pasztor, P-A-S-Z-T-O-R.
No.
My birth date is January the 3rd, 1925.
I'm 71 years old.
Well, I was born in Czechoslovakia, and the name of the place is Bardejov.
But as an infant, we moved to Hungary, and the name of the city is Satoraljaujhely.
And that's where I was brought up until the Germans took us.
Yes.
It was a very nice Jewish community, and life was fairly good.
It was quite a nice place.
It was on the Czechoslovakian border.
Not very big in the way American towns are.
But for a small country like Hungary, it was quite a nice town.
About 25,000 people lived in there.
Not really.
Mostly, we had the wine industry.
It was the region of the Tokaj wine.
Well, we had quite a comfortable home.
And I had one brother.
I had my own room.
And my parents were very lovable, wonderful, wonderful people.
It actually-- it was like three houses, one next to the other.
We lived in the second one.
My father's name was Eugene Pasztor, P-A-S-Z-T-O-R.
And he was a very educated man.
He served in World War I.
He was a decorated general, and he was highly respected in the community.
First, he-- we had a bank with my grandfather.
And then when things sort of started getting bad, they started take away Jewish businesses.
And the first thing was taking away a bank.
He went into the insurance business.
Yes, he was, but he was not a [?
Hasidish ?] man, just a normal religious man who kept the holidays and the Sabbath.
And we lived a very normal modern religious life.
Oh, he indirectly was because he was one of the leaders of the town, but not particularly.

I mean, he wasn't a Zionist because he felt that he fought for the Hungarian country, and he was highly decorated and we had quite a good life [? there. ?] He didn't feel it was necessary even to think of going somewhere else.
My mother's name was Margaret Pasztor, P-A-S-Z-T-O-R.
I think she was a beautiful, wonderful woman.
When things got [SIGHS] tough, she opened a salon for [? knit clothes ?] to help my father make our life better.
She was very talented.
She painted beautiful paintings.
Our home was full of her paintings.
She was a very lively woman.
She taught me all the sports-- the ice skating, swimming, skiing.
But unfortunately, she got sick, and she died of cancer in 1943.
Oh, yes.
She taught me a lot.
She always said, my dear daughter, you have to know everything.
You have to know how to cook, how to bake, how to clean in order to keep a good house when you grow up.
And I can thank her for a lot of things what I know.
Oh, yes, I do, especially the traditional recipes what we used to have for the Sabbath and for certain holidays because, you know, every holiday had certain special recipes.
Well, painting.
That was mostly her interest, beautiful painting.
Unfortunately, I couldn't find anything when I came back.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Yes.
I had one brother.
He was a young boy.
[SIGHS] His name was Steven Pasztor.
And he started school.
He was in high school when things got bad.
And unfortunately, he had to wear the yellow band on his arm.
He was only 13 years old.
It was good four years.
Yes, he was younger.
I only had one grandparent, a grandmother, my mother's mother.
She was the only one I really knew.
And she was a wonderful, very religious, pious woman.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Her name was Lea Ehrman.
No.
In the beginning, I went to the-- a religious school, the Jewish school.
And later on, when I had to go into high school, I had-- the-- the first two years, I went to the nuns, which was called the [HUNGARIAN].
And after that, things sort of started getting bad.
They really didn't like the Jewish children, the-- the Jewish students, so I had to leave that and go into public school.
Public school wasn't too bad at the beginning, but then it started getting tough.
But anyway, I was able to finish it.
And then I registered to the college which we had in town.
I went two years there.
But unfortunately, I had to stop because things were-- everything turned over in the city, and Jews couldn't go anymore.
Besides, my mother got very sick.
It was the time when she was dying.
So things started getting very rough for us.
We did, but not too many.
Few.
Yes, definitely.
Matter of fact, in public school we had some teachers who were quite anti-Semitic.
Well, they made a difference between the Jewish students and the non-Jewish students.
They had more privileges than we did.
Well, where you sat, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was your marks.
You knew that even though you did very well, you would never get the same mark and so forth.
Bad.
Well, my parents encouraged me not to pay attention.
We have to go to school.
And unfortunately, they explained it to me and to my brother that things are not as good as they used to be.
Oh, yes.
I enjoyed sports and-- all kinds of sports what my mother taught me.
Then later on when things were really tough, you know, I tried to help my mother in the business, give her a hand because it was hard for Jewish people to get help.
And that was about all.
Oh, that was beautiful.
What I remember-- the beautiful table set for Friday night and waiting for my father to come home to make kiddush and have the special food.
We always had special food what we did not have during the week.
Also, I positively remember the feeling of Erev Yom Kippur, when the family got dressed special and we all went together to the synagogue.
It was a very special feeling.
Oh, yes.
That was quite an excitement.
We had to empty all our pockets from our clothes.
We had to take all our books outside and air them [? for ?] God forbid should be any crumbs in it.
And it was cooking and baking.
Passover always had a special smell in the house, the clean smell, and the special foods which we ate only Passover.
And then, of course, we had a beautiful Seder.
The table was set.
And when things were getting tough, my father used to come home from the synagogue and always brought quite a few people to our table.
These people were people who escaped, smuggled through the border from Czechoslovakia.
Because things were bad already there, we heard.
And they were sort of getting people together, and they tried to escape.
Well, we felt terrible for them.
We tried to help them as much as we could.
But somehow, because of my father's position, we never thought that it could happen to us.
We heard about Hitler.
And [? oh, ?] news was very hard to get because we weren't allowed to listen to radios.
And the only way we used to get news, some people would listen to the BBC late at night and come to the synagogue and tell it to the other people.
So when my father came home from the synagogue, the first question was always, so what's new?
What did you hear in the synagogue?
Not really, for two reason.
First of all, my father was a special person who had special privileges.
And second of all, my mother got sick, so it was very difficult.
We never thought about leaving.
We always thought that my father's privileges will protect us.
[SIGHS] Well, it's very difficult to say because somebody who really wants to make plans in one's life was interrupted with the bitterness of anti-Semitism.
They never could think of the future, never knew what will happen tomorrow because not only the Nazis were all over, but the Hungarians took over their customs.
And they became very anti-Semitic, and they had special-- like [? all ?] the Iron Guards, and they were also beating up Jews.
So at that time, we had only one thing to think of, hoping that tomorrow will be a better future and we'll be able to think of the future.
Yes, definitely.
Well, don't forget, I was only 16, 17 years old, and things were really bad.
We were just hoping that this will go away like a bad dream and then normal life will come back.
And then I'll be able to continue my schooling and whatever comes after.
Well, first of all, [SIGHS] like my father was struggling to make a living because Jews weren't really welcomed everywhere.
Second of all, my mother-- my mother's salon, where she was, on the main street in a beautiful, big place had to move because Jews weren't allowed to have businesses in the main street.
My brother had to wear a yellow band.
And we were always afraid to go out at night that we will find these Iron Guards who will go after you and beat you.
It started really between '40, '41, something like that.
Only little bits of news what my father used to hear, you know, from the BBC late at night.
That time we did not have to because my father was privileged because he was a high officer in the Hungarian Army-- before that, I mean.
He was in the reserves, really.
That first of all, you couldn't have businesses on the main street.
The children in the high schools had to wear the yellow band, the boys.
Then when my mother passed away, we weren't allowed to bury her in the regular cemetery because it was too close to the road.
And the cemetery was on the side of the mountain.
We had to take her all the way up.
And then there were many other things.
We were afraid to go on the streets the minute it got dark and very uncomfortable feelings.
Up in the mountainside, not where the regular cemetery was.
Yes.
But we couldn't take her on the main street.
Funerals had to go on the side streets.
We only had private doctors.
We had our own doctor, yes, who was also Jewish.
At the very beginning.
But after, when she got towards the last four-- four to six months, she wasn't able to, and I had to run the business.
That was one of the reason I couldn't continue my schooling.
Oh, well, I just knew one thing.
We were terribly sad by my mother's sickness, and I just felt that my duty was to carry on.
I didn't have any other thoughts.
Yes.
We-- many-- you couldn't see many because they were in hiding.
But we did hide also quite a lot of girls who came over.
And during the day, they would stay in our home when nobody was home.
And at night, we would hide them in the business and close it.
And this is how they survived as long as they were there.
And later on, they were able to get certain papers and help them to get some money and go up to Budapest.
From Czechoslovakia.
See, we lived on the border.
Ujhely ?] was on the border.
Young girls, yes.
Not really.
I don't know.
I just-- we only had girls.
Perhaps there were-- there were really men, too, because my father, as I mentioned before, would bring them home for Friday night and Shabbat.
But we only had girls.
Oh, definitely.
Definitely.
We had to be very careful.
That's why we used to take them over to the business late at night when it got dark and watching that nobody should see us on the street.
They would have put us in jail.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
Yes, it did.
Matter of fact, when things sort of were tough already, the Jewish boys were not accepted into the army, but they had like a labor force or something like that.
And then they called my-- my father into the army, and he was their general.
He was a really-- their commander, I should say.
And he took care of them until-- which was about a year.
And after a year, they took these boys to Siberia.
I guess that's when the Germans made a pact with the Russians or something like that.
Anyway, they took them away.
But they did not serve in the regular army.
They were not trusted.
Well, I just heard that it was quite hard labor.
No, he came home.
He went back to the reserves.
And that was one of the reason why we never thought something could happen to us.
Yes, he was.
But work was different then.
It wasn't like you went into the office in the morning and came back at night.
Things were very difficult.
Oh, things were hard.
And it was sort of hard to get food all together for everybody, but especially for the Jews because in certain stores we weren't allowed to shop.
And mainly, we went to the market where the peasants brought in their goods.
Yes.
Yes, the synagogues were open.
Yes, we did-- for a while.
